question: What is the primary role of a client device in a network?

question: What does it mean for a network device to be described as online?

question: What defines a local resource in networking?
choice_a: A file hosted on a cloud storage service
choice_b: A resource accessible only via VPN tunnel

explanation: A local resource resides on your own device; a remote resource lives on another networked system.

question: What distinguishes a partial mesh from a full mesh topology?

choice_c: Partial mesh uses only wireless links

question: What characterizes a mesh topology in general?

choice_d: All devices connect to one central switch
explanation: Mesh topology creates multiple interconnections between nodes so data can travel via alternative paths if one link fails.

question: What type of WAN link uses a provider or carrier circuit for a committed short-distance connection between sites?

question: What does a client-to-site VPN provide to a remote worker?
choice_a: A fiber uplink directly to the office building
choice_b: Encrypted access to internal network resources from a remote location over the internet

question: What general term describes any signal disturbance that degrades communication quality on a network?

question: What causes optical signal degradation in a fiber cable?

choice_c: Optical medium quality declining due to physical bends; damage; or excessive distance

question: In network operations what does it mean when a device or link is described as down?
choice_a: The device is physically rack-mounted but not yet configured

explanation: Down means off; failed; or under maintenance — the device or link is not passing traffic.

question: What is the primary value that networking provides to an organization?
choice_a: Eliminating the need for IP addressing schemes
choice_b: Sharing internet access; files; resources; programs; and user communication channels
choice_c: Reducing the physical count of servers in a data center

explanation: The core value of a network is enabling sharing — internet; files; printers; storage; applications; and communication.

question: What does the shared resource model enable in a networked environment?
choice_a: Each user has a private printer and personal storage with no sharing
choice_b: Printers and storage are centralized and accessible by multiple users simultaneously
choice_c: Resources are distributed to every device to eliminate a single point of failure

question: How does joining a shared network environment affect a user's endpoint independence?
choice_a: Users gain complete control over all shared resources and can set their own policies
choice_b: Users lose full endpoint independence as network policies; sharing; and admin controls govern behavior
choice_c: Endpoints become completely isolated from one another by default

question: What does EMI stand for in the context of network cabling?
explanation: EMI stands for Electromagnetic Interference — noise from motors; power lines; and other electrical sources that degrades signal quality on copper cabling. RFI is the related but separate term for radio-frequency noise specifically.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What is the primary function of a firewall in a network?
choice_a: Assign IP addresses to hosts automatically
choice_b: Connect wireless devices to the wired LAN
choice_c: Forward frames between devices using their MAC addresses
choice_d: Filter network traffic based on policy rules allowing or blocking connections
explanation: A firewall enforces access policy — it allows or blocks traffic by IP address; port; protocol; and connection state. DHCP assigns IPs; a WAP connects wireless devices; and a switch forwards frames by MAC.
aspect_key: never expose endpoints directly to the internet without firewall protection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What does a WAP provide that a standard wired switch cannot?
choice_b: Wireless connectivity for devices that lack an Ethernet port
choice_c: Firewall filtering for inbound internet traffic
choice_d: DHCP address assignment for all connected clients
explanation: A WAP (Wireless Access Point) provides the radio interface that lets wireless clients join the network. A wired switch only serves devices connected via Ethernet cable.
aspect_key: nic; twisted-pair cable; switch; wap; router; and firewall
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: Which component physically interfaces a computing device to its network medium?
explanation: The NIC is the hardware that physically connects a device to the network medium — wired or wireless. Without a NIC the device cannot send or receive network frames at all.
aspect_key: nic; twisted-pair cable; switch; wap; router; and firewall
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What does it indicate when a network interface is reported as up?
choice_a: The interface has been assigned a public IP address
choice_b: The interface is operational and capable of passing traffic
choice_c: The interface is receiving a firmware update
choice_d: The interface is configured for half-duplex mode only
explanation: An up state means the interface is active and operational. Down means it is off; failed; or administratively shut down — not that it is configured or has a particular IP type.
aspect_key: up means operational; down means off; failed; or under maintenance
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What does MAN stand for in the network-size classification system?
explanation: MAN stands for Metropolitan Area Network — it spans a city or metro area; larger than a single-building LAN but smaller than a cross-country WAN.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: In a hub-and-spoke WAN design what is the operational role of the hub site?
choice_a: It is one equal branch office among many in the design
choice_b: It acts as the central node through which all spoke-to-spoke traffic must pass
choice_c: It distributes wireless signals to all remote spoke sites
choice_d: It serves as the backup site that activates only on failure
explanation: The hub is the central routing point — all branch traffic including communication between two branches must route through the hub. Spokes do not connect directly to each other.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What distinguishes a remote resource from a local resource in networking?
choice_a: Remote resources are always encrypted while local ones are not
choice_b: A remote resource resides on another networked system while a local resource is stored on your own device
choice_c: Remote resources require a dedicated WAN link to access
choice_d: Local resources are only accessible when the device is offline
explanation: A local resource is on your own device — its hard drive or memory. A remote resource lives on another system and is accessed over the network. The distinction is about where the data physically resides.
aspect_key: local is on your device; remote resides on another networked system
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: Which four functions does a wireless router combine into a single composite device?
choice_a: Router; DHCP server; DNS server; and firewall
choice_c: Router; switch; DNS server; and WAP
choice_d: Firewall; hub; WAP; and DHCP server
explanation: A wireless router integrates a router (inter-network forwarding); firewall (traffic filtering); WAP (wireless access); and switch (wired LAN ports). Confusing it with a standalone router misses the other three roles.
aspect_key: common small-network device combining router; firewall; wap; and switch ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What does the network value model identify as the core reason organizations build networks?
choice_a: Eliminating the need for IP addressing across the enterprise
choice_b: Reducing the physical count of servers in the data center
choice_c: Enabling sharing of internet access; files; resources; programs; and communication
choice_d: Providing guaranteed quality of service to all endpoints
explanation: The network value model describes why networks exist: shared internet access; shared files and storage; shared printers and resources; shared programs; and shared communication channels all multiply the utility of the infrastructure.
aspect_key: sharing internet access; files; resources; programs; and user communication channels
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What does the dedicated-server model mean?
choice_a: Each user owns a personal server for private file storage
choice_b: A server whose primary task is providing network services to other devices
choice_c: Any workstation that temporarily shares a folder to the network
choice_d: A server that handles only one type of traffic such as DNS
explanation: A dedicated server's sole focus is serving the network — it does not run user workloads. This contrasts with peer-to-peer where a workstation doubles as a server alongside user tasks.
aspect_key: server is optimized for network services as its primary task
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What is the security baseline rule regarding endpoints and direct internet exposure?
choice_a: Endpoints can be exposed to the internet if they run antivirus software
choice_b: Endpoints are safe on the internet as long as they have a private IP address
choice_c: Endpoints must never be directly exposed to the internet without firewall protection
choice_d: Endpoints only need protection when they store sensitive data
explanation: The security baseline requires a firewall between every endpoint and the internet. Direct exposure lets any internet source attempt connections — antivirus and private IPs are not substitutes for a firewall.
aspect_key: never expose endpoints directly to the internet without firewall protection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/MASTER LIST/Master List.md

question: What type of network medium is twisted-pair cable?

question: What does the internet represent in networking terms?

choice_c: A special type of LAN switch
explanation: The chapter defines the internet as the global network of networks.

question: When a small office grows beyond the built-in wired ports on its wireless router what is the usual next step?

explanation: The chapter notes that growing networks often add a dedicated switch when router port count is not enough.

question: Which statement best describes the hybrid network model?

explanation: A hybrid model mixes dedicated servers with some peer-to-peer resource sharing.

question: Why can maintenance windows affect users in a shared network?
choice_a: Servers and shared resources may go offline temporarily

choice_c: It enables fiber on copper ports

question: Which Cisco SFP option from the Week 1 physical-media extension would best match an existing copper horizontal run in an office?
explanation: The copper MGBT1 module is the Cisco Fast Ethernet SFP intended for Cat5 copper runs.

question: Which Cisco SFP option would best fit a short multimode fiber closet-to-closet link?

question: Which Cisco SFP option would best fit a longer single-mode fiber run between buildings?

question: What basic hardware enables a device to connect to a network medium?
explanation: A network interface card provides the hardware needed for network connectivity.

question: What is one major advantage of fiber in electrically noisy areas?
choice_b: It is immune to EMI and RFI
explanation: Fiber uses light instead of electrical signaling so it is not affected by EMI or RFI the way copper can be.
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/practice quiz 1.md

question: Why is a network more than just devices being physically plugged in?
choice_a: It also exists to exchange information and share resources in an organized way
choice_b: It requires a server in every room
choice_c: It only matters if internet access is present
choice_d: It is defined by MAC addresses only
explanation: Week 1 defines a network as connected devices exchanging information and using shared capabilities.

question: Why might a company keep some peer sharing even in an environment that mostly uses dedicated servers?
choice_a: Real networks can use a hybrid model
explanation: The Week 1 reading says hybrid environments are common and combine dedicated servers with some peer sharing.

question: Why is direct internet exposure dangerous for endpoints even in small environments?
choice_a: It invites unwanted inbound traffic without proper filtering
choice_b: It changes a LAN into a MAN
explanation: The Week 1 security baseline says endpoints should not be directly exposed to the internet without firewall protection.

question: Why does the patch-cable lab belong in Week 1 fundamentals?
choice_a: Physical connectivity is part of basic network operation before higher-layer study
choice_b: A patch cable configures routing tables

explanation: Week 1 includes building a patch cable because physical-layer connectivity is a foundational networking skill.

question: What Cisco-documented behavior makes full-duplex Ethernet more efficient than half-duplex on a point-to-point link?
choice_b: It can send and receive simultaneously without shared-medium contention
choice_c: It forces every link to 100 Mb/s
explanation: Full duplex improves efficiency because both directions can operate simultaneously without shared collision behavior.
source_path: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/support/docs/lan-switching/ethernet/10561-3.html

question: What Cisco-documented symptom category should make an engineer suspect duplex mismatch before blaming IP configuration?
choice_a: Layer 1 link is up but throughput and errors are abnormal
choice_d: The MAC address begins with FF
explanation: Cisco's Ethernet guidance highlights duplex mismatch as a performance problem even when the physical link appears up.
source_path: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/support/docs/lan-switching/ethernet/10561-3.html

question: What Week 1 physical-media lesson is reinforced by Cisco's support for both copper and fiber SFP modules?
choice_a: One switch platform can support different media choices
choice_b: All switch ports must use the same cable type
choice_c: Only copper is practical in enterprises
choice_d: Fiber and copper use identical interference properties
explanation: Cisco's SFP lineup reinforces the Week 1 lesson that media selection is a design choice based on environment and distance.

question: What design idea from Cisco Auto-MDIX best extends the Week 1 patch-cable topic into modern switching?
choice_a: Modern switches can often compensate automatically for straight-through versus crossover mistakes on supported copper links
choice_b: Patch cables no longer matter at all
choice_c: Auto-MDIX works on every fiber optic link
choice_d: Console rollovers are replaced by Auto-MDIX
explanation: Auto-MDIX extends the basic cable lesson by showing that many modern copper ports can automatically adapt to the needed pair arrangement.
